justification: Bird correctly identifies the question as out of scope and escalates it, but still populates the full domain_analysis JSON structure (business_context, bounded_context, ubiquitous_language) and includes business_impact, confidence.level, and other domain-template fields. The rubric requires no acceptance_criteria/business_rules/invariants produced (met — those arrays are empty), but also requires conciseness and no domain analysis output. The response pads with the full structured template rather than a brief deflection. Additionally, Bird provides the technical answer in the escalation (BST insertion complexity, red-black tree explanation), which the rubric flags as unsolicited technical content. The deflection intent is clear, but the execution still uses the domain-analysis format rather than a short refusal.

observations.text: Correctly identifies the question as pure CS theory with no domain relevance
Escalation clearly flags out_of_scope and recommends redirecting to a general-purpose assistant
business_rules, acceptance_criteria, and edge_cases are empty — no invented domain rules
Still populates domain_analysis.business_context and the full structured template instead of a brief text refusal
Provides the technical answer (O(log n), red-black tree invariants) in the escalation — rubric penalizes unsolicited technical content

confidence.level is set (50) and domain_analysis is populated, which the fail rubric specifically flags — though the content is a deflection rather than a real analysis, the structure suggests domain processing occurred
